coredata.dc:title: An integrated simulation and experimental study of calendering process in water-based manufacturing of lithium-ion battery graphite electrode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The Society of Manufacturing Engineers.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The Society of Manufacturing Engineers.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yu
Su, Boman
Yuan, Chris
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries produced from water-based manufacturing processes are favored for their environmental and economic advantages, while currently sacrificing some technical performance. This paper reports an integrated study on the calendering process of water-based manufacturing of lithium-ion battery graphite electrode, aiming to improve electrochemical performance of the manufactured lithium-ion batteries. The interactive mechanism between the calendering process and the porous microstructure of the produced graphite electrode was systematically investigated. It reveals that the graphite electrode with 40 % of porosity produced under 79,509 N/m calendering force achieves an optimal 333 mAh/g of specific capacity and 91.2 % of capacity retention after 100 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water-based manufacturing
Anode electrode
Calendering force
Lithium ion battery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of calendering force on the graphite anode.
Evolution of the calendering force during the 1st second of the calendering process for different porosities.
SEM images of water-based graphite anode electrodes with different porosities a). 50 % porosity b) 40 % porosity; c) 30 % porosity; d) 10 % porosity.
Cycling performance and first coulombic efficiency of the graphite anode half-cells with different porosities.
Rate capability of the graphite anode half-cells with different porosities.
The average thickness of graphite electrodes with different porosities.

head.ce:title: An integrated simulation and experimental study of calendering process in water-based manufacturing of lithium-ion battery graphite electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, OH, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, OH, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries produced from water-based manufacturing processes are favored for their environmental and economic advantages, while currently sacrificing some technical performance. This paper reports an integrated study on the calendering process of water-based manufacturing of lithium-ion battery graphite electrode, aiming to improve electrochemical performance of the manufactured lithium-ion batteries. The interactive mechanism between the calendering process and the porous microstructure of the produced graphite electrode was systematically investigated. It reveals that the graphite electrode with 40 % of porosity produced under 79,509 N/m calendering force achieves an optimal 333 mAh/g of specific capacity and 91.2 % of capacity retention after 100 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries, characterized by their high energy density, long cycling life, and light weight, have been widely used in portable electronics, medical devices and electric vehicles (EVs). In a lithium-ion battery, an anode and a cathode are manufactured and paired up with separator in between to function as an electrochemical device. Both the anode and the cathode are manufactured following a series of manufacturing processes including mixing, coating, drying, calendaring, slitting, etc. In mixing of materials for manufacturing the anode and the cathode, binders play important roles for providing interparticle contact and facilitating the adhesion of the electrode laminate to the current collectors during the following coating process. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is the most widely used binder material in current manufacturing of lithium-ion batteries due to its excellent electrochemical stability and strong adhesion properties [1]. Other promising binder materials, such as PVA [2], PVC [3], PPA [4] and PHBV [5], have also been explored for use in electrodes. However, the limited solubility of most organic binders in aqueous solvents requires the use of organic solvents, which are toxic for humans and raises great environmental concerns. As a result, the recycling system is necessary to mitigate environmental impacts and enhance the sustainability of lithium-ion battery manufacturing by recycling waste NMP.

To address these concerns from the use of NMP solvent, water-based manufacturing processes, which replaces the toxic NMP with water and replaces PVDF with corresponding aqueous binders, have been developed. These aqueous binders, compared to their organic counterparts, offer similar electrochemical stability and adhesion performance but at a reduced cost [6]. Additionally, the use of water as the solvent, with a lower boiling point, reduces the drying temperature, thereby, increasing the energy efficiency of the drying process. Moreover, water, as an eco-friendly solvent, eliminates the requirement for the solvent recovery system in the later vacuum drying process. Considering the stability of active materials in water, the water-based electrode manufacturing processes are primarily used in anode electrode fabrication.

The calendering process, a critical step in electrode manufacturing, reduces electrode thickness and increases areal density. The calendering process raises the energy density of lithium-ion batteries and extends their cycling life by increasing the coating density and improving particle-to-particle contact, particularly for thick electrodes [7–10]. A calendering model has been established by Meyer et al. to quantify the evolution of electrode mechanical properties during the calendering process, including coating density, compaction resistance, and coating porosity [11]. The calendering process also smooths the rough surfaces to prevent short circuits and eliminates the potential punctures to the separators [12]. However, the calendering process decreases the porosity of the electrode, causing the lithium-ion pathway to become longer and more tortuous and hindering lithium-ion migration from the electrolyte to the electrode [13]. Additionally, a low porosity extends the electrolyte wetting time and decreases the amount of electrolyte absorbed in the electrodes [14]. Recent studies have concentrated on the calendering effect of cathode materials on the battery performance. Zheng et al. have studied the calendering effect on the physical and electrochemical properties of NMC111 cathode electrodes [15]. Heubner et al. have investigated the effect of thickness and porosity on Li-ion insertion kinetics of NMC622 cathode electrode [16]. As the counterpart of the cathode electrode, the microstructure and morphology of the anode electrode are influenced by the calendering force, which significantly impacts fast charge capability and determines the battery lifespan. Several models have been proposed in recent research to simulate the calendering process for both cathode and anode electrodes [17–19]. Additionally, other researchers have explored the combination of calendering with innovative techniques, such as laser structuring, to further improve the performance of graphite anodes [20]. However, there remains a lack of in-depth experimental investigation into the evolution of calendering forces during the process and their effects on the microstructure of water-based anodes in lithium-ion batteries.

The purpose of this study is to investigate the correlation between the calendering force, electrode porosity, and the electrochemical properties of water-based graphite anode electrodes across a range of porosities. A non-linear solid mechanics model was developed and applied to simulate the variation of the calendering force during the calendering process. The initial capacity, coulombic efficiency, cycling performance, and rate capability of electrodes with different porosities are discussed in this study. The optimized porosity is also identified in this study, providing a new insight for the design of high mass loading water-based anode electrodes with a high overall performance for lithium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode manufacturing and battery cell assembly
ce:section.ce:para: To manufacture the anode, artificial graphite (X4–2, AME Energy) was used as the active material and was mixed with carbon black (Super C65, MTI), CMC (Sigma Aldrich), and SBR (MTI) in a mass ratio of 94:1:2.5:2.5 to form slurries. The slurries were cast on the 10 mm copper foil using a doctor blade method at a speed of 10 mm/s and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the produced anodes were calendered to various thicknesses by a roll-to-roll calendering machine (MSK-2150, MTI) to obtain different internal structures and porosities. After, the electrodes were then cut into disc shapes, with a mass loading of approximately 8 mg/cm2. To further reduce the moisture content before battery cell assembly, a second vacuum drying process was performed on the manufactured electrodes. Finally, the 2032-type battery cells were assembled using the calendered electrodes as the anode and Li metal foil as the counter electrodes. The electrolyte was 1.2 M LiPF6 in EC: EMC: DMC (1:1:1 wt%), and the separator was Celgard 2500 membrane.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic of the calendering on the coated graphite electrode, and the corresponding change in the electrode thickness and porosity before and after the calendering process. The porosity of the electrode was determined by the volume ratio of electrode materials and pores. Based on the internal structure of the graphite electrode, the porosity of the electrode can be defined and calculated by the following equation [21]:
(1)
P = 1 −
W V
r Gr
ρ Gr
+
r CB
ρ CB
+
r CMC
ρ CMC
+
r SBR
ρ SBR
where:
•
P is the electrode porosity.
•
W is the weight of the electrode laminate, excluding the copper foil.
•
V is the volume of the electrode laminate, excluding the copper foil.
•
r Gr
,
r CB
,
r CMC
, and
r SBR
denote the weight ratios of graphite, carbon black, CMC, and SBR in the graphite electrode, respectively.
•
ρ Gr
,
ρ CB
,
ρ CMC
, and
ρ SBR
are the physical densities of the artificial graphite, Super C65, CMC, and SBR, respectively.

According to the Eq. (1), the porosity of uncalendered electrodes was 50 %. To investigate the calendering effect on the performance of graphite anode, the manufactured graphite electrodes were compressed to achieve various porosities of 40 %, 30 %, and 10 %, respectively. The change in porosity, resulting in the elastic deformation of the electrode, was maintained within the ±2 % range. Table 1 shows the thickness and actual porosities of the graphite electrodes after the calendering process, relative to the calculated porosity values.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was employed to observe the morphologies and porosities of the manufactured graphite electrode at 1 kV. Electrochemical tests were carried out using the Neware battery tester. The assembled battery cells underwent 3 formation cycles at 0.1C. Following the formation step, the cells were charged and discharged in the potential range of 0.01–1.5 V at 0.5C for 100 cycles. The cycling performance test followed a CC-CV (constant current-constant voltage) protocol. The rate capability of the calendered electrodes was assessed using a CC (constant current) protocol at current densities of 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted using VersaStat 3F potentiostat (AMETEK, USA) over a frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz at an amplitude of 10 mV.

In the calendering process, the graphite electrodes are compressed to a predetermined thickness by adjusting the gap width between the double rollers of the calendering machine. Given that the deformation of current collectors is negligible, the reduction of the electrode thickness during the calendering process is primarily attributed to a decrease in void space and an increase in the compactness of the conductive network within the electrode laminate. These factors significantly influence the electrode performance, including electronic conductivity, electrode adhesion, lithium pathway, etc. However, the evolution of calendering force during this process is complex, owing to the properties of the electrode laminate. Consequently, a comprehensive investigation of the calendering force is crucial to optimize the electrode performance.

To study the relationship between the electrode porosity and calendering force, the evolution of the calendering force was simulated using COMSOL over a one-second duration. The porosity of the uncalendered electrode is 50 %, at which point the calendering force is zero. Three different output layer thicknesses were computed, corresponding to the electrode porosities of 10 %, 30 %, and 40 %, as listed in Table 1. The comprehensive computational details, including governing equations, boundary conditions, and modeling parameters, are provided in the supplementary materials. Fig. 2 illustrates the evolution of the calendering force throughout the first second of the process for varying thicknesses. In each scenario, the calendering force exhibits a pronounced increase within the initial 0.1 s, followed by a steady state thereafter. By the end of the first second, the calendering force spans a range from 46,736 N/m to 200,420 N/m. Noted that calendering process can be typically finished within one second. The results showed a higher calendering force is required to achieve a lower thickness, consistent with prior literature [22]. Initially, the material undergoes elastic deformation, transitioning to plastic deformation upon surpassing the yield stress.

Fig. 3 illustrates the morphologies of the calendered graphite electrodes with different porosities. The uncalendered electrode with 50 % porosity exhibited a pronounced porous microstructure throughout the laminate. The size and number of pores in the uncalendered electrode are larger than those in the calendered electrodes with lower porosities. Carbon black, CMC, and SBR are uniformly dispersed among graphite particles, forming a loose conductive network. These large pores facilitate rapid electrolyte absorption, reducing the wetting time and enhancing the lithium transportation within the electrode. However, large gaps between the electrode laminate and the current collector can be observed in the uncalendered electrodes, which decreases the contact area and increases the electronic resistance. Under a calendering force, the pore size of the 40 % porosity electrode was slightly decreased, maintaining the porous structure similar to that of the uncalendered electrode. The calendering force compacted the electrode, improving the contact interface, smoothing the electrode surface, and reducing the surface area. In the case of a 30 % porosity electrode, both the size and quantity of pores were significantly reduced by the calendering force, resulting in a denser electrode laminate. As the calendering force increased, the electrode laminate adhered firmly to the current collector, improving the contact interface. The surface condition was further optimized by the large calendering force, which is beneficial to the formation of a stable SEI layer. When the electrode was calendered to 10 % porosity, the porous structure in the highly compact electrode was nearly diminished, leading to a tortuous lithium pathway. The extremely high calendering force applied on the electrode eliminated gaps between the electrode laminate and the current collector. Moreover, the morphology of graphite particles was also changed due to inter-particle collisions under the substantial calendering force, impacting the physical and electrochemical properties of the electrode. The absence of pores also reduces the electrolyte absorption amount and prolongs the electrode wetting time.

In current commercial lithium-ion batteries, electrode porosities range widely from 20 % to 45 % [23]. The electrode porosity is designed to achieve either high energy performance or power performance. For electric vehicles, however, both energy density and power density are crucial, particularly for the graphite electrode. A high energy density in the graphite anode can extend the driving range, while a high power density can facilitate fast charging/discharging. Consequently, identifying an optimized porosity by adjusting the calendering force that simultaneously obtains an optimal energy and power performance is quite important.

Fig. 4 demonstrates the first coulombic efficiency (inset) and cycling performance of graphite half cells using electrodes with varied porosities. The first coulombic efficiency serves as a critical parameter for evaluating electrode quality and quantifying lithium consumption during the formation of the Solid Electrolyte Interface (SEI) layer. The coulombic efficiency of the electrodes with the porosities of 10 %, 30 %, 40 %, and 50 % are 93.4 %, 92.3 %, 91.8 %, and 90.5 %, respectively. As observed, the first coulombic efficiencies are decreased, as the porosities of the graphite electrode increase from 10 % to 50 %. A lower specific surface area of the anode compressed by a higher calendering force facilitates the formation of a thin and compact SEI layer, resulting in a lower active lithium consumption and a high coulombic efficiency.

The electrode with porosities from 50 % to 10 % showed capacities of 327.9, 333.0, 325.1, and 320.6 mAh, respectively. The 40 % porosity electrode showed the highest capacity due to the optimization of both physical and electrochemical properties of the graphite electrode by the calendering process. For porosities from 50 % to 10 %, the capacity retentions were 88.6 %, 91.2 %, 91 %, and 86.6 % after 100 cycles. This highest capacity retention of 40 % porosity in the graphite electrode is attributed to the porous microstructure providing a short lithium pathway and enhanced contact at the interface. As the porosity decreases to 30 %, the tortuous lithium pathway becomes the limiting factor, reducing lithium transportation speed from the electrolyte to the graphite particles. Due to the highest calendering force applied on the electrode with 10 % porosity, the porous structure was eliminated, and the particle morphology was changed, decreasing the available capacity and capacity retention rate.

Fig. 5 shows the rate capabilities of the graphite electrodes under different porosities. At lower current densities, the electrodes with different porosities had similar capacities. The electrode with 40 % porosity exhibited the highest rate capability of 330.2, 320.3, 266.8, 184.1, 106.5, and 75.2 mAh/g at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, 1C, 2C, and 3C, respectively. At a current density of 1C, the electrode with 40 % porosity retained 83 % of its initial capacity, whereas the 10 % porosity counterpart showed a capacity retention of only 50 %. Due to the high calendering force, the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of the 10 % porosity electrode was largely increased, highly determined by the tortuosity of the lithium pathway, and emerged as the main constraint on the rate performance. Hence, the electrode with 10 % porosity, possessing the highest tortuosity, exhibited a capacity fade rate of 93 % at 3C. When the current density was reverted to 0.1C, electrodes of all porosities showed a full capacity recovery.

An integrated simulation and experimental study have been conducted on the calendering process in water-based manufacturing of lithium-ion battery graphite electrode. The results show that the calendered graphite electrode with 40 % of porosity produced under 79,509 N/m calendering force achieves the highest 333 mAh/g of specific capacity and 91.2 % of capacity retention after 100 cycles. The calendering process increases the volumetric energy density by reducing the void spaces within the electrode and mitigates contact resistance between the electrode laminate and the current collector. However, the porous structure of the laminate is decreased by the calendering force, leading to a tortuous lithium pathway and sluggish lithium transportation. Thus, a compromise between electrode cycling performance and rate capability is obtained in this study. While the results derived from this study depend on the types of active/inactive materials and electrode loading, they offer insightful guidance for refining the manufacturing parameters of lithium-ion batteries, especially for calendering process, to achieve the highest electrochemical performance.

Yu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Boman Su: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software. Chris Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Science Foundation (Award # 2101129) of the United States is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmapro.2024.09.076.

J Electrochem Soc
